fund.name: ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is the anchor of Minerva's plan — a 23-year-old fund from ICICI Prudential that spreads your money across stocks, bonds, and gold automatically. It has delivered ~20% per year over 5 years with lower volatility than a pure stock fund. Since it holds less than 65% in stocks, all gains are taxed at your income-tax slab (30%), but its diversification makes it ideal for capital preservation. It handles its own rebalancing, so you don't have to do anything actively.

fund.name: SBI Multi Asset Allocation Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: A second multi-asset fund from SBI — a different fund house — so your money isn't concentrated with one company. SBI has a 20-year track record managing this kind of blended fund. It adds diversification across fund managers and investment styles. Like the ICICI fund, gains are taxed at your slab rate, but the lower market swings (~8.5% volatility) make this a good fit for your capital-preservation priority.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is a 25-year-old hybrid fund — one of the oldest in India — that keeps roughly 65–80% in large-cap stocks and the rest in bonds. Because it stays above 65% in stocks, gains here are equity-taxed: only 12.5% tax on gains above ₹1.25L held over a year. That's a meaningful tax advantage for someone in the 30% slab. This fund gives your portfolio a slightly higher growth engine while still being much less volatile than a pure stock fund. Think of this as your 'growth-with-cushion' allocation.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC Limited

allocations.rationale: This is your pure debt (bond) allocation — your safety net. It invests in corporate bonds maturing in 3–4 years and has returned ~10–12% over time with very low ups and downs (volatility under 2.5%). This portion is earmarked mentally for your ₹50L healthcare contingency fund — it needs to be stable and accessible. Important tax note: gains are taxed at your 30% slab, so the ~10% return becomes ~7% post-tax. That's still solid for capital-preserving money. From a different fund house (Aditya Birla), adding further diversification.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Think of this as your emergency parking spot. ₹3 crore goes here initially — this is ultra-safe money that earns slightly above a savings account rate (~5.5–6.3%) and can be withdrawn in one business day. This chunk represents your liquid buffer: 2–3 years of living expenses + the ₹50L healthcare fund base. No SIP needed — just park the lumpsum and use it when life requires it. Gains are taxed at your slab but since returns are low and this money is for near-term use, the priority is safety and liquidity over returns.

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1: Get your direct plan account ready**
Open a free account on Kuvera (kuvera.in) or MFU (mfuonline.com). These platforms give you access to direct plans — the same funds but without any middleman commission, saving you 0.5–1% yearly. If you prefer a bank interface, Kotak, HDFC, or SBI also offer direct mutual fund investments.

**Step 2: Complete your KYC (one-time)**
You'll need your PAN card, Aadhaar, and a photo. KYC takes 10–15 minutes online on CAMS or Karvy portals. Do this first — you cannot invest without it.

**Step 3: Deploy the ₹2 crore lumpsum in tranches**
Don't put ₹2 crore in on Day 1. Here's how to do it safely:
- Month 1: Park ₹3 crore in ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund (the safety buffer) immediately
- Months 1–4: Move ₹25L per month from the overnight fund into the other 3 funds (ICICI Multi-Asset, SBI Multi-Asset, ICICI Equity & Debt, and Aditya Birla Debt) — roughly ₹40–50L per month across them. This spreads your entry and avoids putting everything in at a market peak.
- Month 5 onwards: Your ₹3 crore lumpsum is fully deployed

**Step 4: Set up your 5 monthly SIPs**
- ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund: ₹30,000/month
- SBI Multi-Asset Allocation Fund: ₹20,000/month
- ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund: ₹20,000/month
- Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan: ₹15,000/month
- Total: ₹85,000/month (keep ₹15,000 as monthly buffer in your bank)

**Step 5: Open a Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP) in year 5**
When you retire in 5 years, switch your ICICI Equity & Debt Fund into an SWP mode — this lets you draw a fixed monthly income (say ₹50,000–₹80,000/month) directly to your bank account like a salary. This is the most tax-efficient way to create retirement income from mutual funds.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check that all SIPs are running and the lumpsum has been deployed in tranches. Log into Kuvera and confirm all 5 fund accounts show transactions.

After 6 months: Check your overall portfolio value. Don't panic if it's slightly below your lumpsum investment — this is normal if markets dipped during entry. The overnight fund portion should be stable.
After 1 year: Compare each fund's 1-year return against its own category average. If any fund has underperformed its category by more than 4–5%, flag it for review — but don't act immediately.

Year 3 (2027): This is your key pre-retirement checkpoint. Check if the total portfolio is on track for your inheritance goals (₹4 crore total = ₹2 crore per child). If markets have been strong, consider moving more allocation toward the debt/overnight fund to lock in gains.

Year 4 (2028): Begin planning your retirement income setup. Decide the SWP amount you'll need monthly. Contact a tax advisor about spreading redemptions across years to keep annual gains under ₹1.25L per year for equity funds to minimise tax.

Year 5 (2029, retirement): Activate SWPs in the hybrid funds for monthly income. Shift a larger portion (25–30%) of the corpus into the Overnight/Debt fund so 2–3 years of living expenses are always liquid and safe.

Year 10 (2034): Final review. Evaluate whether inheritance goals are met. If corpus has grown beyond target, consider partial withdrawal to gift to children while you are alive — gifts to children are tax-free in India.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
**Annual check (every January):** Look at how each fund's value has grown as a percentage of your total portfolio. Your target mix is roughly: 50% multi-asset/hybrid (ICICI + SBI multi-asset), 20% aggressive hybrid (ICICI Equity & Debt), 15% medium-term debt, and 15% overnight fund.

**When to rebalance:** If any category has moved more than 5 percentage points from the target — say the hybrid funds have grown to 60% because markets did well — move some money back to the debt/overnight fund to restore the balance. Do this once a year, not more.

**Tax-smart rebalancing:** Since your debt and multi-asset gains are taxed at 30%, prefer doing rebalancing by directing new SIPs toward the underweighted fund rather than selling and reinvesting. Selling triggers tax; redirecting new money does not.

**Post-retirement shift (year 5 onwards):** Gradually reduce your allocation to the ICICI Equity & Debt Fund (aggressive hybrid) from 20% to 10% and move that into the Overnight or Medium-Term Debt fund. At retirement, your ideal mix shifts to: 30% multi-asset hybrid, 10% aggressive hybrid, 30% medium-term debt, 30% overnight/liquid. This is your capital-preservation mode.
plan.rationale:
Minerva is 55, retiring in 5 years, has ₹2 crore already saved, no debt, children settled — this is an enviable financial position. The plan is built around one core principle: **don't lose what you've built**.

**Why this mix?** With ₹2 crore corpus + ₹1L/month SIP over 10 years, we're targeting a ₹4–5 crore portfolio at age 65. That comfortably funds: a ₹50L healthcare buffer (held in safe debt), ₹4 crore inheritance (₹2 crore each child), and ongoing monthly income through SWP.

**Why no small-cap or pure equity?** At this life stage, a 30–40% crash in markets would wipe out years of compounding. The plan avoids all small-cap exposure entirely, keeps large-stock exposure only within hybrid funds (where bonds act as cushion), and holds 30% in pure debt/overnight instruments at all times.

**Tax context for a 30% slab investor:**
- The multi-asset funds (ICICI + SBI) are slab-taxed — 30% on all gains. Their ~9–10% post-tax return is still attractive given their low volatility.
- The ICICI Equity & Debt Fund is equity-taxed (only 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L held over 1 year) — much better tax treatment. This is why we kept 20% here despite slightly higher risk.
- The debt fund and overnight fund are also slab-taxed but serve capital-preservation purposes, not growth.

**The inheritance goal is achievable:** ₹2 crore corpus growing at 10.5% for 10 years = ~₹5.45 crore. Even in a bear scenario (6.5% returns), the corpus reaches ~₹3.77 crore — comfortably covering both children's inheritance and the healthcare fund.

**Funds are spread across 4 different companies** (ICICI Prudential, SBI, Aditya Birla, with ICICI also in the overnight fund) — so no single company's troubles can derail the plan.
risks: Stock market drops: The hybrid funds hold 30–65% in stocks. In a bad year (like 2020 or 2008), your portfolio could temporarily fall 15–20%. This is normal and has always recovered over 2–3 years — but it's important to know this will feel uncomfortable.

Debt fund risk: The Aditya Birla Medium Term Fund invests in corporate bonds. Rarely, a company can fail to repay its bonds (this happened with some funds in 2019–2020). ABSL is a large, experienced debt manager but the risk is not zero. If this concerns you, you can swap this for a Government Securities (Gilt) fund which has zero default risk but more interest-rate sensitivity.

Interest rate changes: Debt funds and bond-heavy hybrid funds lose value when the RBI raises interest rates. In rising-rate environments, expect 1–2% temporary dips in your debt fund. This reverses as rates stabilise.

Tax law changes: Indian mutual fund tax rules have changed twice in the last 5 years. The current equity tax rate (12.5%) and slab-taxed categories could change in future budgets. Consult a CA every year at tax filing time to stay updated.

Longevity risk: At 55, planning only to 65 may not be enough. If you live to 85 or 90 (very plausible), you need the corpus to last 30+ years. Consider keeping SIPs running even after retirement rather than deploying everything in income mode.

Inflation: Healthcare inflation in India runs at 10–12% per year — higher than general inflation. Your ₹50L healthcare fund today may not be enough in 15 years. Review this every 3–4 years and top it up as needed.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.0):** The equity sleeve of this plan contains zero passive/index instruments. All equity exposure is embedded within actively managed hybrid and multi-asset funds (ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset, SBI Multi-Asset, ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt). No Nifty 50, Nifty Next 50, or any other index fund/ETF is recommended. Score: fully active equity = 0.0.

**turnover_score (0.75):** Rebalancing is explicitly restricted to once per year (every January), only triggered when allocations drift more than 5 percentage points. The plan also instructs investors to redirect new SIPs rather than sell-and-reinvest to avoid tax — a deliberate low-turnover approach. However, there is a structured glide path from aggressive to conservative over 5 years (post-retirement shift), which adds some intentional but infrequent activity. Overall, this is closer to buy-and-hold than active trading. Score: 0.75.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.35):** The plan mentions direct plans and the savings from avoiding commissions (0.5–1% yearly), and recommends platforms like Kuvera/MFU for direct plan access. However, cost is not the primary selection criterion — fund house diversification, volatility profile, tax treatment, and capital preservation dominate the rationale. Expense ratios (0.55–1.0%) are noted but not foregrounded as the key driver. Score: moderate cost awareness = 0.35.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.2):** The plan exhibits meaningful fund-specific analysis: fund track records (23-year, 25-year histories), returns across 1y/3y/5y horizons, volatility figures, tax treatment differences between hybrid categories, fund house diversification logic, and even a warning about credit risk in the ABSL medium-term fund. This is clearly not broad-market passive exposure — it reflects active fund selection reasoning. However, it stops short of individual stock/sector analysis (which is delegated to the underlying fund managers). Score: 0.2 (active, research-driven selection).

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.7):** The plan has a 10-year primary horizon (age 55 to 65) with review checkpoints at year 1, 3, 4, 5, and 10 — mostly milestone-based rather than opportunistic. The SIP structure, lumpsum tranche deployment to average entry, and SWP planning for retirement income all reflect patient, long-term thinking. The post-retirement glide path and longevity risk mention suggest even longer-term thinking. The year-3 checkpoint ("if markets have been strong, consider moving more to debt") introduces a mild tactical element, but overall the orientation is long-term. Score: 0.7.

**portfolio_activeness_score (0.1):** No risk metrics (beta, alpha, tracking error, information ratio) are available for any of the five funds. Relying on category and rationale: all equity-containing funds are actively managed multi-asset and hybrid funds with discretionary stock/bond/gold allocation decisions made by fund managers. The ICICI Multi-Asset and SBI Multi-Asset funds dynamically allocate across asset classes; the ICICI Equity & Debt Fund maintains active equity selection with a 65–80% equity mandate. The debt funds (ABSL Medium Term, ICICI Overnight) are also actively managed. There are no index-tracking instruments anywhere in the portfolio. This is a fully actively managed portfolio across all sleeves. Score: 0.1 (genuinely active throughout).

pqs.reasoning:
## Goal Alignment (0.78)
The plan broadly aligns with Minerva's goals: capital preservation, retirement income via SWP, healthcare contingency fund earmarked in overnight/debt instruments, and a projected corpus that can fund ₹4Cr inheritance across two children. The SWP setup in year 5 is explicitly planned, and review checkpoints address the inheritance goal (₹4Cr check in Year 3). However, some gaps exist: (1) The plan references a "₹2 crore corpus" in the rationale but the profile states ₹2 Cr (20,000,000 INR) existing corpus — the lumpsum allocations total ₹20Cr correctly, but the rationale's language is inconsistent in places. (2) The ₹50L healthcare fund is only loosely earmarked across the overnight fund (₹3Cr) without a precise ring-fencing or separate vehicle. (3) No SIP step-up is proposed despite the investor having ₹1L/month surplus with only ₹85,000 deployed — the ₹15,000 buffer is noted but not formally structured. (4) The 10-year horizon is addressed, but longevity risk beyond 65 is mentioned only in the risk section, not structurally addressed in allocation.

## Diversification (0.65)
The plan spans multiple asset classes (equities via hybrid, bonds, gold indirectly through multi-asset, overnight instruments) and three fund houses (ICICI Prudential, SBI, Aditya Birla). However, ICICI Prudential appears in three of the five funds (ICICI Multi-Asset, ICICI Equity & Debt, ICICI Overnight), creating significant fund-house concentration. The two multi-asset funds (ICICI and SBI) carry overlapping mandates and likely similar underlying holdings, reducing effective diversification. There is no international/geographic diversification and no REITs or gold-specific fund despite the investor's long horizon and capital-preservation goal. The debt sleeve is thin (15% medium-term + 15% overnight), and no government securities or gilt fund is included despite credit risk being flagged as a concern for the ABSL fund.

## Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.70)
The conservative investor is largely served: no small-cap, no pure equity, hybrid funds dominate. The 10.5% base projected return is reasonable for the blend. Bear scenario of 6.5% leading to ~₹3.77Cr is realistic and covers goals. However, 20% allocation to an Aggressive Hybrid fund (65–80% equity) is at the higher end for a "conservative" investor aged 55. The plan acknowledges a possible 15–20% temporary portfolio decline, which could be uncomfortable given the capital-preservation mandate. The ABSL Medium Term fund carries credit risk (flagged in risks) which isn't entirely in keeping with conservative preferences. The overnight fund is appropriate for liquidity but ₹3Cr (15%) earning ~5.5% pre-tax is a drag in the long-term plan. No formal downside hedging or floor strategy is proposed.

## Internal Consistency (0.62)
Several inconsistencies undermine the plan: (1) The ICICI Multi-Asset and SBI Multi-Asset funds are categorised as "Conservative Hybrid" in the fund metadata, but the plan rationale calls them "multi-asset" and describes them with moderate risk — the category label mismatch (these are Multi-Asset Allocation funds, not Conservative Hybrid) creates confusion and the plan doesn't reconcile it. (2) The rationale correctly notes that multi-asset funds (holding <65% equity) are slab-taxed at 30%, yet the plan still allocates 50% of the portfolio to them as the "anchor" — without adequately weighing this tax disadvantage against alternatives. (3) The setup phase mentions deploying "₹2 crore lumpsum" but total lumpsum across funds is ₹20Cr (₹2Cr = ₹20,000,000). The language oscillates between "2 crore" and "20 crore" in a confusing way, suggesting the plan may have a ₹2Cr vs ₹20Cr confusion. (4) Total SIPs add to ₹85,000 but the plan says "5 SIPs" in the setup — there are only 4 SIP-enabled funds (the overnight fund has no SIP). (5) The rebalancing guideline post-retirement targets 30% overnight/liquid but the plan currently only parks 15% there, with no mechanism described for how the shift will be funded.

## Tax Efficiency (0.55)
The plan shows partial tax awareness. Positives: it correctly identifies the ICICI Equity & Debt Aggressive Hybrid as equity-taxed (12.5% LTCG) and explicitly calls this out as a tax advantage for a 30% slab investor, justifying the 20% allocation. It also mentions tax-smart rebalancing via SIP redirection rather than redemption, and advises spreading redemptions to stay under the ₹1.25L LTCG exemption annually. However, significant gaps remain: (1) 50% of the corpus is in Multi-Asset funds taxed at the 30% slab — the plan does not explore whether arbitrage funds or a higher allocation to Aggressive Hybrid could deliver similar risk-adjusted returns at equity tax rates. (2) No ELSS is considered, and while the investor at 55 may have limited 80C appetite, this isn't explicitly reasoned through (₹1.5L ELSS per year over 5 years would be meaningful). (3) The plan correctly flags that multi-asset funds are slab-taxed, but doesn't model the post-tax return differential comprehensively to justify why these are still preferred over better-taxed equity alternatives. (4) The SWP tax efficiency in retirement is mentioned but not detailed (e.g., equity LTCG exemption planning per year).
